titleEn: Nothing Startled Me but Separation and Its Cruelty

verses.sadr: فكأن من قد غاب جاء مواصلي

translationEn: Nothing startled me but separation and its injustice, so I obeyed it while fate hangs from my reins. Leave the blamers to prolong their reproach — I am a friend to blame and blamers alike. The beloved draws near even when his dwelling is far, through a phantom that visited in dreams. It is as if one who was absent came to visit me, and I wave to him in greeting. I have endured hardships and calamities until I ascended to the most honored station. I overpowered the champions of battle until they lay wounded and slain by the strokes of my sword. Longing has stirred — pour me a cup of aged wine until the sun sets beneath the dark.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: startled me, alarmed me
separation, parting
those who reproach and blame (plural)
to prolong, to go on at length
phantom, apparition (of a beloved in dreams)
wild enduring hardships, extraordinary trials
aged wine, wine kept long

context: A poem blending ghazal tenderness with martial fakhr. Antara opens with the shock of separation but immediately channels it through his warrior resolve — enduring blame, dreaming of the absent beloved, and recounting battlefield victories. The final image of wine poured until sunset reflects the pre-Islamic convention of drowning sorrow in drink, used here ironically against his own sorrow.

titleEn: He Answers the Conscience's Subtle Signals

verses.sadr: وإني عزيز الجار في كل موطن
وقد خيروني كأس خمر فلم أجد
verses.ajuz: سوى لوعة في الحرب ذات ضرام
عوابسا عليها كرام في سروج كرام

verses.sadr: ولا تذكرا لي طيب عيش فإنما
وفي الغزو ألقى أرغد العيش لذة
verses.ajuz: وفي المجد لا في مشرب وطعام
جرى لأبعد شأو من بعيد مرام

translationEn: He answers the conscience's subtle signals and spares you the need for whip or bridle. I have the might of one with muscular arms, drowsy like a lion defending his cubs. I honor my neighbor in every land and ennoble myself against lowering my station. I abandoned towering houses for the gleam of bright swords beneath the canopy of dust. They offered me a cup of wine but I found nothing but burning anguish in the fire of battle. I shall depart from you and not visit your dwellings, seeking them instead in every cloak of darkness. I pursue enemies with every swift and noble warrior and every lion-bold in combat. I forbade sleep until I led them — stern-faced riders on honored horses in honored saddles. They brandish spears in their hands as if they had drunk pure wine from the breast. When leveled for the thrust they looked like stars guided by full moons. And white swords in the shadow of the dust-cloud, like drops of lutes in the darkness of clouds. Sing to me the neigh of horses — that is my music, and the shimmer of blood is my cup-companion. Rest my saddle on the burning sand — that is my resting place, and the fluttering of banners my tent. Do not remind me of a pleasant life, for attaining hopes is my health and my sickness alike. In raiding I find the richest pleasure of life and in glory, not in drink and food. Why should I accept humiliation when my sharp sword is bold at necks and never blunt? I have a horse that imitates the winds when it runs to the most distant goal and furthest aim. Is it injustice — while my spear stands by me and my sword — and abasement — while my glory holds my reins?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: signals of the conscience/instinct
drowsy, lying in wait (said of a lion)
his cubs (lion's)
sharp bright swords
dust, thick battle-dust
swift noble warrior
lion, bold warrior
blunt, ineffective (of a sword)

context: A long fakhr poem showcasing Antara's full warrior identity. The opening image of a horse so attuned to its rider it needs no whip is one of the great horse descriptions in Arabic poetry. The poem then enumerates all martial virtues — guarding neighbors, scorning comfort, wielding sword and spear — and climaxes in the paradox that his glory itself holds his reins.

titleEn: The Women Cry Out in Fear of Captivity

verses.sadr: يا جفوني إن لم تجودي بدمع
لا رفعت الحسام في الحرب حتى

translationEn: The full moon was eclipsed at its completion and its light was hidden, turning back to darkness. The bright planets set and disappeared, and the luminance of the horizons became thick dust — when they said that Zuhayr had fallen slain, grief pitched its tent among us and settled. Time gave him the cup of death to drink, and thus does time water the doves. He was my support and my equipment in calamities — he was my armor, my long spear, and my sword. O my eyelids, if you will not be generous with tears I shall forbid sleep to you forever. I swear by Him who gives death and life and who governs souls and bodies: I shall not raise the sword in battle until I leave the people as bones in the wilderness. O sons of Amir, you shall meet a lightning-bolt from my sword that streams blood in torrents. And the women cry out in fear of captivity and weep over the young orphans.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: eclipse of the full moon
bright planets, luminous stars
thick dust, obscuring haze
cup of death (fate)
long flexible spear
wilderness, open desert
in torrents, flowing freely

context: An elegy and revenge oath for a fallen comrade named Zuhayr. The cosmic imagery of the full moon eclipsing and stars disappearing is a classical ritha convention signaling the magnitude of the loss. Antara swears a solemn oath before God to avenge the death, threatening Banu Amir with devastation — the women crying and children orphaned.

titleEn: Halt, My Two Friends at Dawn, and Greet

verses.ajuz: وعوجا فإن لم تفعلا اليوم تندما

verses.sadr: على طلل لو أنه كان قبله
أيا عزنا لا عز في الناس مثله
verses.ajuz: على عهد ذي القرنين لن يتهدما
علوت بها بيتا من المجد معلما

verses.sadr: إذا ما ابتدرنا النهب من بعد غارة
ألا رب يوم قد أنخنا بدراهم

verses.ajuz: من الناس إلا راحت بالدم ملئت دما

translationEn: Halt, my two friends, at dawn and give peace, and turn aside — if you do not do so today you will regret it. Halt at a ruin, whose effaced traces would have spoken if only they could speak before now. O our glory, no glory among people matches it — from the age of Dhul-Qarnayn it shall never crumble. When the tribe of Abs marches behind me with spears, I raise for them a banner of glory most high. You see them mustering swift horses and long spears, high-necked, mounted on roan and jet-black steeds. When we race to the spoils after a raid we stir up dust as black as pitch with our hooves. Many a day we have camped among enemies I straightened my sword and my balanced spear against. No people ever raised a banner to meet us in battle without their banner returning soaked in blood. We annihilated their gathering with our spears and we struck down their champion and shattered him — with every fine-edged Indian sword, a blade that is resolute when it meets the blow. Its point splits the skulls of the armored men and slices from the champions hand and forearm.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: ruins of an abandoned dwelling
effaced, faded traces
swift noble horses
horse hooves
very dark, pitch-black (of dust)
their champion, their ram (leader)
tip of a sword or spear
to slash, cut through

context: A classic qasida structure: opening with the ruins of the beloved's dwelling, pivoting to tribal fakhr. The address to two companions at ruins (atlal) is the canonical pre-Islamic opening. The martial boasts that follow glorify Banu Abs's raids and Antara's own swordsmanship, concluding with a vivid description of the lethal blade.

titleAr: عليك أيا عبيلة كل يوم سلام
titleEn: Upon You, O Ubaylah, Each Day a Peace
translationEn: Upon you, O Ubaylah, each day a peace — peace upon peace upon peace. The phantom of Abla came to me in sleep and kissed me three times through the veil. Then it bade me farewell and left in me a burning I conceal, yet it blazes in my bones. If only I could be alone with myself and quench with tears the fire of my longing. O daughter of Malik, how can I find solace when my love for you goes back to weaning? How can I ever seek closeness to you when around your tent stand lions of the thicket? By the right of your love, I shall treat my heart with nothing but patience, O daughter of the noble. Until I climb the steps of glory by the thrust of the spear or the stroke of the sword. I am the slave whose story you have heard — I tended the camels of my people from the time of my weaning. I go from dawn to nightfall and I sleep among the tent-ropes. I submit myself to Abla from the excess of my longing and make her my sole concern in the world. I obey the commands of her father while love has seized my reins entirely. I have accepted her love willingly and against my will — shall I win her before death? If she reproaches my blackness it is my pride, for I am a knight descended from Ham. I have a heart harder than mountains and my fame spreads like the fragrance of musk. The wonder of it is: I hunt lions by force and prey on wild beasts like insects — yet the gazelles of al-Sa'd set a snare for me, and the doe-eyes of al-Sharba and the lavender conquer me. By your father's life, I shall never forget her love even if love for her grinds my very bones. Upon you, O Ubaylah, each day a peace — peace upon peace upon peace.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: phantom, dream-image of the beloved
the veil worn over the mouth
hidden anguish, burning passion
weaning (from infancy)
thickets, dense reed-beds (where lions dwell)
mountains, firm anchors
to prey on, to devour as a predator
small crawling creatures, vermin

context: One of Antara's tenderest ghazal poems, built around a triple-repeated refrain of 'peace upon peace.' The dream-vision of Abla kissing him is contrasted with the waking reality of being forbidden from her. The poem contains Antara's signature self-deprecation about his slave status and dark skin, transformed into pride through warrior identity.

titleAr: أنا في الحرب العوان غير مجهول المكان
titleEn: In the Raging War I Am Not Unknown

verses.sadr: أنا في الحرب العوان غير مجهول المكان
verses.ajuz: أينما نادى المنادي في دجى النقع يراني

translationEn: In the raging war I am not unknown — wherever the caller calls in the darkness of battle-dust he sees me. My sword and my spear are the two witnesses to my deeds — that I thrust my adversary while he is brave of heart. I give him the cup of death to drink and the drink is near from him. I kindle the fire with my valor and quench it with my courage. I am a scowling lion with no equal in creation. The spear was made for my hand and the Indian sword — they were with me in the cradle, resting on my chest as two companions. When the earth turns red like rose-oil and the blood flows over it in a crimson scarlet — and I see the horses falling headlong through the wide plain — then pour me a drink, not from a cup but from blood red as crimson dye. Let me hear the melody of swords until they delight me. The sweetest of sounds to me is the ring of the Indian blade and the creak of the spear out loud in battle on the day of the thrust — and the war cry of men as it descends upon the champions.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: raging, fierce (war that has been fought before)
battle-dust, clouds of dust in combat
my spear (qanah)
crimson, deep red (royal purple-red dye)
flat open plain, level desert
Indian sword, blade from India
creak, grinding sound

context: A bravura fakhr poem in short rajaz-like verses, building to the famous declaration that Antara's music is the ring of steel and the war cry. The image of being rocked to sleep in the cradle with sword and spear — his two companions from birth — is one of his most celebrated self-portraits.

titleAr: أحبك يا ظلوم فأنت عندي مكان الروح
titleEn: I Love You, O Unjust One, You Are to Me Like the Soul
translationEn: I love you, O unjust one — you are to me like the soul within the body of a coward. But if I were to say you are in the place of my soul, I would fear for you the sudden thrust of the spear.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: one who is unjust, the beloved who wrongs the lover
the place of the soul, as essential as the soul
the sudden first thrust of the spear, the opening attack
the coward

context: A two-verse gem of the ghazal tradition. Antara uses a warrior's logic to express love: placing the beloved in the position of his soul would endanger her in battle. The paradox — that loving her too openly endangers her — is characteristic of Antara's fusion of martial and romantic registers.

titleEn: O King Whose Palms Stand in Place of Rain

verses.sadr: يا قبلة القصاد يا تاج العلا
verses.ajuz: يا بدر هذا العصر في كيوانه

verses.sadr: ما ليس يوصف أو يقدر أو

verses.ajuz: من كل فن لاح في أفنانه

verses.sadr: ملك إذا ما جال في يوم اللقا

translationEn: O king whose open palms stand in place of rain in his times — O direction of petitioners' faces, O crown of glory, O full moon of this age in its station. O shamer of the rain-star with his generosity, O rescuer of the grieved from his sorrows. O dwellers of Abs's abodes, I have received from Kisra and from his beneficence what cannot be described or measured or do justice to by any tongue. A king who has gathered all the ranks of glory with an elevated honor that dwells in his palace. A lord through whom the age and its people are ennobled, and time itself has gained pride from his crowns. When he strikes, all creatures fear his might — and even the lion fears him at the sight of him. He who makes justice manifest in his days through his qualities, and equity in his lands. I spent the evening in a fertile quarter of his, strolling in it and in his garden. I saw his blessing overflow and its water telling of his gifts and the generosity of his fingers. In a spring encampment where spring gathered in its quarter every art that shone in its branches. And birds of every kind sang openly that all of time is in submission to his rein. A king who, when he roams on the day of encounter, leaves the enemy standing in bewilderment at his affair. Victory is among his companions before all others, and fortune and favor are among his supporters. I shall therefore give thanks for his deed publicly and shall joust with the horsemen in his arena.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: those who set out to seek a patron, petitioners
Saturn (used to denote the highest station)
the rain-star, the luck of the sky bringing rain
royal hall, vaulted palace hall (Sasanid term)
branches (of trees), also varieties and types
his sitting companions, his court intimates
the assembly, the public gathering

context: A court panegyric (madh) addressed to a king described in terms recalling the Sasanid Kisra (Khusraw). This is unusual for Antara, who rarely composed formal panegyric, preferring self-praise. The lush garden scene and catalog of royal virtues — generosity, justice, martial power — follow the classical madh structure.

titleEn: When My Adversary Demands His Debt
translationEn: When my adversary demands a debt from me I repay the debt with the Rudaynian spear. The edge of the sword satisfies us all and judges between you and me with justice. You have misjudged my worth, O sons of the despicable — but the people of the two horizons know it well. The hand of calamities has not broken my pillar, nor has the finger of my doom reached me. I have risen with my sharp sword and the tip of my spear above the horizon of Suha and the two Farkadayn stars. I left the dueling ground in the middle of a desert as he scraped his cheek and temples in the dust. How many a horseman at my sword's touch has lain with a shattered skull and blood-dyed hands — while the eagles of doom wheel above him and the ravens of separation hop around him. And another flees in terror of my presence, shedding tears from both eyes. I shall annihilate your gathering with my patience, and my burning anguish will be quenched and my eye satisfied.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: made by Rudayna, a woman renowned for crafting spears (attributive)
the two horizons, east and west
vicissitudes of fate, calamities of time
al-Suha, a dim star (used to denote the highest height)
the two Farkadayn stars near the North Star
with a shattered crushed skull
my burning anguish, the fire of my grief

context: A powerful fakhr poem structured around the metaphor of 'debts repaid with spears.' The catalog of defeated enemies — skulls shattered, blood on hands, ravens circling — contrasts with the enemy's cowardly flight. The final verse promises the speaker's anguish will be quenched only through complete victory.

titleAr: يا طائر البان قد هيجت أشجاني
titleEn: O Bird of the Ban Tree, You Have Stirred My Sorrows

verses.sadr: يا طائر البان قد هيجت أشجاني
إن كنت تندب إلفا قد فجعت به
زدني من النوح واسعدني على حزني
verses.ajuz: حتى ترى عجبا من فيض أجفاني

verses.sadr: وقف لتنظر ما بي لا تكن عجلا
وطر لعلك في أرض الحجاز ترى
verses.ajuz: ركبا على عالج أو دون نعمان

verses.sadr: ناشدتك الله يا طير الحمام إذا

translationEn: O bird of the ban tree, you have stirred my sorrows and increased my longing — O bird of the ban tree. If you are mourning a companion you were bereft of, then the same thing that grieved you through separation has grieved me. Add more of your lamentation and help me in my grief until you see a wonder from the overflow of my eyelids. Pause to see what ails me, do not be hasty — and beware yourself of the breaths of my inner fire. Then fly, perhaps in the land of Hejaz you will see a caravan at Aalij or near Nuaman, traveling with a young woman whose tears pour down in longing for a far homeland and neighbors. I adjure you by God, O dove, when you one day see the laden camels of the tribe, cry for me — and tell them: 'We left one prostrate whose tears have run dry as he weeps in scarlet blood.'

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: bird of the ban (moringa) tree, a symbol of separation
stirred my sorrows, made me grieve
to mourn, to lament
intimate companion, beloved
separation, parting
my inner fire, the blaze of my passion
lament for me, announce my death (naah)

context: A ghazal in the classical mould of addressing a bird as messenger — here the ban-tree bird and the dove. The poet asks the bird to fly to Hejaz and find his beloved's caravan, then report back that he lies prostrate weeping tears of blood. The symmetry of the bird's grief and the poet's grief is a hallmark of the genre.

titleEn: For Whom Are These Ruins at al-Raqmatain That Grieve Me

verses.ajuz: غراب به ما بي من الهيمان

verses.sadr: ينوح على إلف له وإذا شكا
ألا يا غراب البين لو كنت صاحبي
عسى أن نرى من نحو عبلة مخبرا
وقد هتفت في جنح ليل حمامة
فقلت لها لو كنت مثلي حزينة
وما كنت ذي دوح تميس غصونه
أيا عبل لو أن الخيال يزورني
لئن غبت عن عيني يا ابنة مالك
دعوا الموت يأتيني على أي صورة أتى

translationEn: Whose are these ruins at al-Raqmatain that grieve me — the hands of decay have wrought them into a likeness of my own state. I stood there while longing wrote lines with the pens of my tears on the effaced traces of my heart. I questioned the ruins about Abla and a raven answered me — afflicted as I am with mad wandering love. It mourns a companion and when it complains, it complains with sobbing not with the speech of a tongue. It laments from excess of hidden anguish, so I answered it with the sighing of a heart forever trembling. O raven of separation, if you were my companion we would have traversed God's lands in our wandering, hoping to find a messenger from Abla's direction, in whatever land, in whatever place. A dove cried out in the wing of the night — trilling, lamenting the reversals of time. I said to it: if you were as grief-stricken as I, you would weep with overflowing tears. You would not be perched on a swaying-branched tree with feet dyed scarlet. O Abla, if the phantom would visit me once a month that would suffice me. Though you are absent from my eye, O daughter of Malik, your form stands clear before my gaze. Tomorrow the enemies will be among your homes, biting their fingers in grief. Do not think that armies will hold me back when I roam your environs on my horse. Let death come to me in whatever form it comes — I shall show it my battle-stance and my thrust.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: al-Raqmatain, a place name (dual form)
the hands of decay and erosion
mad wandering love, delirious passion
copious unceasing tears, overflow
large spreading tree, tall tree
sides, environs, precincts
my battle-stance, my position in the fight

context: A long ghazal structured around two birds — the raven of separation and the dove of night — as mirrors of the poet's grief. The ruins at al-Raqmatain open the poem in classical atlal mode; the poet converses with birds, rebukes the dove for complaining too lightly, then turns suddenly to a martial threat against enemies near Abla's home.

titleEn: O Dwelling, Where Have the Inhabitants Departed

verses.sadr: يا ردا عبلة أين خيم قومها

verses.ajuz: بكى من وحشة نزلت عليه البان

verses.sadr: يا صاحبي سل ربع عبلة واجتهد
يا عبل ما دام الوصال لياليا
يا طائرا قد بات يندب إلفه
لو كنت مثلي ما لبثت ملونا

verses.ajuz: الذي أفنى ولا يفنى له جريان

translationEn: O dwelling, where have the inhabitants departed — the camel-litters carried them away from us. Yesterday in you the gazelles were at home, and today in your courtyards the ravens are. O echo of Abla, where did her people's tents go when the camels bore them away and they were gone? The thickets of the arak-trees lamented and the ban tree wept from the desolation that settled upon it. O dwelling: the dwellings are the souls of their inhabitants — when they go far, the bodies weep for them. O my companion, question Abla's encampment earnestly, if the abandoned quarter has a tongue. O Abla, how long did union last for nights — until separation afterwards struck us. Would that the dwellings could inform a questioner where the homelands settled with their people. O bird that spent the night lamenting his companion, moaning, bewildered and lost — if you were like me you would not linger so colored and fair, nor would the branches sway with you. How far is the carefree heart from one whose heart is full from the heat of longing's fires. Lend me your wing and borrow my tears — the tears that are spent yet their flowing is never spent — so I may fly asking about Abla, if one like me can fly.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: camel-litters bearing women, the departing caravan
open courtyards, open spaces of a dwelling
thickets, groves (of trees)
toothbrush tree (Salvadora persica), common in the Arabian peninsula
ruined, changed beyond recognition (of a dwelling)
madly distracted, frenzied with grief
hidden burning anguish of love

context: A sustained ghazal built on the classical motif of questioning the abandoned dwelling. Antara addresses the ruins, the echo, the bird, and finally imagines borrowing the bird's wings to fly and search for Abla. The poem crescendos with the poignant image of tears that are always running yet never run dry.

titleEn: Ask, O Abla of the Two Mountains, About Us
translationEn: Ask, O Abla of the two mountains, about us — and what the sons of the foreigners encountered from us. We annihilated their gathering when they came to us, their formations surging like waves of men and jinn. They sought to devour us without being hungry, so we satisfied them with blows and thrusts. We struck them with white sharpened blades that split their bodies — back and belly. We separated the formations from their women — women who surpass the women of the earth in beauty. How many a chieftain has my sword left with palms stained, not with henna but with blood. How many a hero's women I have left weeping, repeating their lamentations over him in sorrow. Hajar saw my thrust and called out: 'Go gently, O son of Shaddad, go gently!' I was created with a heart stronger than mountains, and though mountains may perish, I do not perish. I am the well-built fortress of the house of Abs when the champions erect fortresses. My color resembles the night, yet in my deeds I am brighter than the whiteness of dawn. My horse is my lineage and my father and my mother — my sword and the spear when we trace our pedigrees.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: sons of the non-Arabs, foreigners
sharpened to a keen edge (swords)
to split, to cleave through
with palms dyed/stained (in blood)
go gently, take your time, be measured
the well-built fortress, the strong fortification
the spearhead, the tip of the spear

context: One of Antara's most famous fakhr poems, addressed to Abla as witness of his martial glory. The climactic verse — 'my horse is my lineage, my sword and spear are my father and mother' — is among the most quoted in Arabic poetry, Antara's definitive rejection of the tribal genealogy that denied his legitimacy due to his enslaved mother.

titleAr: ألا يا غراب البين في الطيران
titleEn: O Raven of Parting in Its Flight

verses.sadr: ألا يا غراب البين في الطيران
ترى هل علمت اليوم مقتل مالك
لقد كان يوما أسود الليل عابسا
فلله عينا من رأى مثل مالك
فليتهما لم يجر يا نصف غلوة

translationEn: O raven of parting, in your flight — lend me a wing, for I have lost my fingers. Have you today learned of the killing of Malik and his fall in humiliation and degradation? If it is true, then the stars for his loss will set and after him both moons will plunge. He was a day of pitch-black night, scowling — the calamity of fate feared to strike him. God bless the eye of whoever saw the like of Malik — the prize horse of his people when the horsemen raced. Would that they had not raced even half a furlong, would that they had not been sent out for a wager. Would that both horses had died in some land and that Qays had missed them, never to see them again. For they brought doom and great war that annihilated the nobles of Ghatafan. They brought doom to the killing of Malik, who was noble, generous, of pure bred descent.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the raven of separation (omen of parting)
prize race-horse of the people (literally: hamstrung one)
a bowshot's distance, a short unit of race distance
a wager, a race-bet
doom, destruction, time of ruin
the nobles, the chiefs of the people
of pure bred lineage (horse term used for people)

context: An elegy for Malik, whose death was precipitated by a horse race that led to war — possibly connected to the famous War of Dahis and al-Ghabra, which began over a disputed horse race between Abs and Ghatafan. Antara mourns through the raven-messenger convention and bitterly wishes the fatal race had never taken place.

titleEn: He Was in Battle Defending Its Sacred Trust

verses.sadr: به كنت أسطو حينما جدت العدا
فوا أسفا كيف انثنى عن جواده
فسوف ترى إن كنت بعدك باقيا

translationEn: He was in battle defending its sacred trust and thrusting with every kind of thrust in the charge. Through him I used to strike when the enemies came boldly at me at the dawn of battle with every Yemeni blade. His loss and calamity has toppled my pillar and left my heart in perpetual trembling. Alas — how did he fall from his horse when my sword and my spear were not beside him? A resolute archer shot him with death's arrow — would that when he shot him he had shot me instead. You shall yet see, if I remain after you and time and long years permit me — I swear truly: if you had lived for one glance, your eye would be satisfied when it sees me.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: battle, fierce combat
its sacred trust, what must be defended at all costs
the charge, the attack and return in battle
Yemeni (sword), a blade attributed to Yemen's famous smiths
toppled my pillar, destroyed my main support
resolute, determined, piercing to the bone
your eye would be satisfied/cooled (idiom for being pleased)

context: A continuation of the elegy for a fallen companion — possibly Zuhayr or another close ally. The poem pivots from grief to a vow: if only the arrow had struck Antara instead. The closing verses promise the fallen companion that he will see great vengeance carried out in his name.

titleAr: أرى لي كل يوم مع زماني عتابا
titleEn: I See Myself Each Day Reproached by My Age

verses.sadr: أرى لي كل يوم مع زماني عتابا
ألا يا دهر يومي مثل أمسي

verses.ajuz: فما أدري أبا سمى أم كناني

translationEn: I see myself each day reproached by my age, in distance and in nearness. It seeks to humiliate me and circles me with an army of calamities when it sees me. As though I have grown old, my head has turned white, my endurance diminished and my resolve weakened. But O Time — today is like my yesterday, and I inspire greater awe in those who meet me. Many a distressed man I relieved of his distress with a decisive blow when he called on me. He called out to me while horses were running — I could not tell whether he called my name or surname. I did not stop my ears to hear when he called — instead my tongue made clear to him my reply. I scattered the formations away from him by force with a thrust that outran the Yemeni lightning. I answered his call with nothing but my sword and my spear racing each other in the fray. My answer to him was that I wheeled upon them my pliant-reined horse — with a supple Khattian brown spear and a sharp resolute Yemeni white blade. Many an adversary I left at the battlefield with strips of red flowing on him like crimson dye. I left the birds hovering over him as bridesmaids are guided to a wedding. They are prevented from eating him by a hand and foot still kicking. The constant wear of battle has not weakened my pillar, nor has the hand of time reached me. I have not drawn near to the face of death except as the brave draw near to the coward. Banu Abs have known that I am eager when called to the thrust of spears. And that death is in my hand's command when my fingers grasp the Indian blade. Excellent horsemen of battle are my people when they loop the reins around their fingers. They have slain Luqayt and Ibn Hajar and struck down Hajib and the two sons of Aban.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: calamities, recurring misfortunes
my endurance, my toughness, my bearing of pain
decisive, separating (said of a decisive blow)
Khattian spears, from al-Khatt (a place in Eastern Arabia famous for spears)
strips of cloth or flowing streams (of blood)
crimson, deep red royal dye
the constant practice and wear of war

context: A long fakhr poem structured as a defiant challenge to Time itself. Antara insists he has not aged, not weakened — and proves it with a narrative of answering a distressed man's call in battle. The poem ends with a catalog of enemies his tribe has slain: Luqayt, Ibn Hajar, Hajib, the sons of Aban — names that would have resonated with tribal audiences.

titleEn: Longing Stirred Me and the Yemeni Lightning Moved Me
translationEn: Longing stirred me and the Yemeni lightning moved me, reminding me of the encampments and the singing-places. It kindled in the core of my heart a fire like the strike of my Indian sword. By your life, the spears of Banu Bagheeth do not betray the hands that hold them on the day of thrusting. Nor do their swords turn blunt in battle when the brave man is told from the coward. Rather they strike the army with mighty blows and bury the vultures without burial dishes. They plunge into the terrors of death at the dawn charge in the raging war. O Abla, if you asked my spear about me it would answer you, its tongue freely speaking — that I raided the abodes of Taym with every lion-bold of firm heart. I waded through its dust as horses plunged, my sword and spear racing each other. While men take delight in drinking wine and the wine of the casks hides away their reason — my reason is not hidden by aged wine, nor do I incline to the laughter of the wine-jars. Many a Badr we left lying prostrate, as though wrapped in a crimson robe. I pierced his heart as he turned to flee with a straightened spear of a keen-edged blade. He fell upon the surface of the earth, sprawled, cheek in the dust and fingers blood-stained. We returned and glory is our garment — with it we lord over the people of the age.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the Yemeni lightning (from the south, associated with longing and rain)
singing-places, beloved encampments where songs were heard
they bury the vultures (i.e., kill so many that even the scavengers die)
lion, a powerful warrior
aged wine, repeatedly fermented wine
straightened, made perfectly true (of a spear)
with cheek in the dust, prostrate in defeat

context: The poem opens with the standard ghazal trigger — lightning from the south recalling the beloved's land — then pivots to a full tribal fakhr praising Banu Bagheeth warriors. Antara distinguishes himself from wine-drinkers, boasting that battle is his only intoxicant. The closing verse — 'glory is our garment' — is among his most celebrated lines.

titleEn: I Recalled My Longing After a Long Time
translationEn: I recalled my yearning after a long time and the old madness returned to me. My heart yearned for the Hejaz and its love stirred again after calm. Do men seek Abla from me — men who are least of all in certainty of knowledge? Gently — my deeds are calamities that turn the heads of ages white with terror. How many a night I have mounted a steed in it, yet woke at morning in a sturdy fortress. My left rein called to me and my sword at my right rebuked me: 'Shall a vile wretch take Abla and enjoy wealth and riches instead of you?' How many a noble man complains of a base man, and how many a pure-bred encounters the mixed-blood. My enemies found no fault in me, so they reproached me for a color that shows in the eyes. I have no supporter in hardship but Qays — he is the one who guards me from them. A noble man in calamities I rely upon, just as he selects me for the fierce battles. The rope of one who hopes has become strong — grasp it by the sturdy rope. Of the noble people who are suns that cannot be hidden by darkness. When they witness a battle I would say: lions from supple long spears in a den. O king who has gathered the ranks of glory — I have taken refuge with you, so be my support. You have settled in happiness in a place lofty in worth, without equal or peer. Whoever opposes you does so in severe humiliation, and whoever allies with you does so in manifest glory.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: my yearning, my intense longing
madness, the madness of love
great calamities, weighty affairs
the heads of generations, the peaks of ages
of pure noble white breed (said of camels, extended to people)
long supple spears (watered with experience)
without peer, incomparable

context: A poem that weaves ghazal longing, personal complaint about racial discrimination (his dark skin), and political dependence on a noble patron named Qays. The complaint about being judged by skin color rather than deeds is a recurring and historically significant theme in Antara's poetry, reflecting the lived experience of an Afro-Arab warrior in pre-Islamic Arabia.

titleAr: يا عبل أين من المنية مهربي
titleEn: O Abla, Where Is My Escape from Death

verses.sadr: يا عبل أين من المنية مهربي
verses.ajuz: إن كان ربي في السماء قضاها
وقرا إذا ما الحرب خف لواها

verses.sadr: من كل أروع ماجد ذي صولة
ما استمت أنثى نفسها في موطن
وأغض طرفي ما بدت لي جارتي

verses.ajuz: أن لا أريد من النساء سواها

translationEn: O Abla, where is my escape from death if my Lord in the heavens has decreed it? Many a battalion I covered with another — grey-white, fierce, dreaded for its onslaught. Silent in its equipment, visible in its arms, as though it were a fire blazing in its fierce heat. In it the heroes, sons of heroes — while the horses stumble in battle among their spears — like firebrands in the hands of those who kindle fires when their brilliance dazzles the darkness in their palms. Patient men who prepared every sleek swift horse and every noble she-camel that grew lean and light of flank. Running with the fully-armored in grim urgency — lead-mares complaining of their fatigue and soreness. They bear young men who charge with spears and are steadfast when the war's banner grows light. From every awe-inspiring generous man of might, hardened when tested at the limit. Companions high of nose I dispatched at night when drowsiness had bent their champions. I marched them through the heavy darkness, leading them, until I saw the sun shed its forenoon light. I met a battalion near the noon heat and thrust the first horseman at its vanguard. I struck my lance-duel champion, its ram, and he fell — I drove my horse through its midst and split through it — until I saw the horses, after their blackness, red-skinned, dyed by the wounds of their battle. Stumbling in the thick dark blood, fleeing, trampling the prostrate fallen of the battle. I returned praised, bearing the head of its greatest, and left them as carrion for those who opposed them. No woman has ever valued herself in a situation but her master has paid her dowry in full. Whenever a brother of honor borrowed a price from me as a loan, for him with me there was its double. I lower my gaze whenever my neighbor-woman appears until her retreat conceals her. I am a man of generous nature and noble spirit — I do not follow the soul's persistent desires in its caprice. If you ask Abla of this, she will tell you that I want no woman but her. And I answer her whenever she calls for a great matter and I support her and refrain from what grieved her.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: death, fate (personified)
heroes, champions, fully-armored warriors
those wearing full armor, the fully-armored
the thick heavy darkness, difficult dark terrain
dark congealed blood from wounds
carrion, animals slaughtered and left (plural of jazr)
of generous natural character, easy-natured

context: One of Antara's most comprehensive poems, encompassing warrior identity, tribal ethics, chivalric honor (lowering eyes before a neighbor's wife), and devotion to Abla. The vivid battle narrative of a night march, dawn attack, and victorious return is among the most cinematically detailed in his diwan. The closing verses affirm his chivalry as equal to his martial prowess.

titleEn: I Am Not Abandoning You, But
translationEn: I am not abandoning you — rather I shall strive now that it has reached its ripeness. But the sons of Sawda inherited it and kindled its fire for those who warm themselves at it. And if your war has become a raging second-generation war, I was not among those who brought it on.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: abandoning you, letting you down (khidhlaan)
reached its time of ripeness, come to its full measure
a woman's name; ولد سودة = sons of Sawda, a tribal faction
warmed themselves at it (the fire of war)
a second-generation war, a raging renewed conflict

context: A brief but significant political declaration. Antara asserts his loyalty while distancing himself from responsibility for the war's origins. The term 'harb 'awan' (second-generation war) is a key pre-Islamic concept denoting a conflict renewed after previous bloodshed — here Antara insists he was not among those who ignited it.

titleEn: Halt at the Dwellings and Call Out to Their Expanse

verses.sadr: ما بال عينك لا تمل من البكا
يا عبل قد هام الفؤاد بذكركم
يا عبل إن تبكي علي بحرقة
يا عبل إني في الكريهة ضيغم
verses.ajuz: شرس إذا ما الطعن شق جباها

verses.sadr: يا عبل كم من فارس خليته
يا عبل كم من حرة خليتها
يا عبل كم من مهرة غادرتها
يا عبل لو أني لقيت كتيبة

translationEn: Halt at the dwellings and call out to their expanse — perhaps the dwellings will answer one who calls them. A dwelling from whose courtyards musk wafts and whose yield is aloe-wood and fragrant aromatic. Abla's dwelling whose visiting-place has grown distant from you — by my life, I do not think you will see it. What is wrong with your eye that it does not tire of weeping — is there a soreness in it, or has sleep abandoned it? O my companion, halt the mounts for a moment at Abla's dwelling, asking after its singing-place. But how does one ask a worn-out ruin swept by south winds that erased its blood-red soil? O Abla, my heart wanders madly at your memory and I see my debts as debts whose repayment is not due. O Abla, if you weep for me with burning grief — women have long wept for their men. O Abla, I am in the fierce battle a ferocious lion, savage when the thrust splits its foreheads. The rams of war drew near to the rams, warming themselves at the fire of battle or wading through its blazing heat. The brave man drew near to the brave man and the brown spears were leveled at their different shafts. There I thrust into the battle its horsemen with a thrust that splits hearts and kidneys. Ask the horsemen — they will tell you of my resolve and my battle-stances when I dominate it. I add to it from my war's fire a new flame and stir it until its millstone turns. I charge into them in the blaze of its rays and I am the first arrival to enter its fire. I am the first to strike with an Indian blade that slices through skulls, wanting nothing else. I am the first horseman to plunge into battle and I lead the first horseman who plunges into it. The horses and the horsemen know that I am the old man of wars and its middle-aged and its young. O Abla, how many a horseman have I left in the middle of a hill counting its pebbles. O Abla, how many a free woman have I left weeping and lamenting her husband and her brother. O Abla, how many a mare have I left after her rider dragging her fetters. O Abla, if I met a battalion of seventy thousand I would not fear meeting them. I am Death itself and the son of every death — and the blackness of my skin is its garment and its robe.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: its open expanse, its wide desert plain
sharp-scented aromatic incense, fragrant aloe-resin
a worn-out ancient ruin, a relic of old habitation
ferocious lion
her kidneys (dual); also the inner organs
the elder of wars — veteran, master of battle
death, fate; Antara calls himself Death itself

context: Perhaps Antara's most climactic fakhr poem, addressed throughout to Abla as witness. After the conventional atlal opening the poem builds to an extraordinary rhetorical peak: a fourfold 'O Abla, how many a...' — horseman, free woman, mare — abandoned in his wake. The closing verse — 'I am Death and the son of every death, and the blackness of my skin is its garment' — is the most famous self-portrait in the entire diwan, transforming the racial stigma into cosmic pride.

titleEn: Ask Juhayna About Us — How Did She Spend the Night
translationEn: Ask Juhayna about us — how did she spend the night wandering in terror through her hills? She saw my thrust and turned to flee and set out — while the brown Khattian spears worked in her rear. I left in her land after the human beings nothing but ravens hopping in her open plain.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: Juhayna, an Arabian tribe
to wander aimlessly in fear or madness
her hills, her elevated ground (ruba, plural of raba')
she set out, she lifted her camp and departed
working in her rear, striking from behind as she fled
to hop on one leg (said of ravens and corvids)

context: A compact three-verse fakhr, devastatingly economical. The tribe of Juhayna is left empty of humans, only ravens hopping through the ruins — a powerful image of total defeat. Antara's instruction to ask the enemy themselves for testimony of his power is characteristic of his defiant rhetorical style.

titleEn: With My Sword I Established the Market of Death
translationEn: We met at the day of Sahba' a swift troop — Hanzalites with determination in battle. We met them with sharp swords and lions who do not flee from death. Their leader at that time was a lion, a fierce beast who did not care about calamity. We left him lying in the middle of the flat ground, and here I am seeking to slay the remainder. We went with swords driving them into hidden difficult hills. How many a horseman of them we left — upon him the favors of our swords were decided. Our horsemen are Banu Abs — and we are the lions of war among all creatures. We excel in thrusting with high upraised brown spears and we strike with the Mushrifi swords. We shoe our horses in every war with the blood-stained skulls of chieftains. On the day of giving we bestow all we own of wealth and beautiful herds. We are the just when we judge, and we are the compassionate toward the subjects. We are the equitable when called to the thrusting of Samhari spears. We are the victorious when we charge on the noble Ajwaji horses. We are the kindlers of every war and we enter it with brave hearts. We have filled the earth with fear of our might and the kings of Kisra feared us. Ask all of the Levant about us and the horsemen of the Caesar-kings. I am the slave raised in the abodes of Abs with the pride of the noble defiant soul. Ask al-Numan about me on the day the horsemen of the fierce fire-brotherhood came — with my sharp sword I established the market of death and with my long spear I attained the highest ranks.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: Hanzalites, members of Banu Hanzala tribe
high-raised brown spears, upright in the hands
Mushrifi swords, blades attributed to a place called al-Mushraf
Ajwaji horses, of the noble breed descended from Ajwaj
Samhari spears, extremely hard straight-shafted spears
our power, our might, our domination
a long flexible spear

context: A comprehensive tribal fakhr in rhymed -iyya verses, cataloging all the virtues of Banu Abs: in war, in justice, in generosity, in governance. The anaphoric 'we are the...' structure builds to a crescendo. The penultimate declaration — 'I am the slave raised in Abs's abodes with proud noble spirit' — and the reference to al-Numan's testimony situate the poem in the wider political world of Lakhmid Arabia.

titleEn: O Dwelling of Abla at al-Tawa
translationEn: O dwelling of Abla at al-Tawa — like the return of a tattoo on the pastern of a sacrificial camel. Like the inscription of parchments from the age of Kisra, presented to a dumb stammering foreigner. Is it from the vicissitudes of fate — on the day when Banu Jarm rose high against Banu Adi? When they clashed together you heard among them a sound — nothing but the sound of the Mushrifi sword — and nothing but penetrating arrows that issued from them with a thrust like the ropes of a well. And Tha'l ibn Amr let them down — their Salamanites and al-Jarwali.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: al-Tawa, a place name
tattoo, brand-mark (on camels or as decoration)
the pastern of the sacrificial camel (hady)
dumb, inarticulate, one who cannot speak Arabic clearly
Banu Jarm, an Arabian tribe
penetrating arrows, projectiles that pass through
well-ropes, the ropes used to draw water from a well

context: A shorter poem describing a tribal battle between Banu Jarm and Banu Adi, with a faction (Tha'l ibn Amr's Salamanites) betraying their allies. The opening atlal image comparing the ruins to an old tattoo on a sacrificial camel — and to Sasanid inscriptions — is one of Antara's most visually intricate comparisons.

titleEn: May God Requite the Ancient Crumbling Ruins

verses.ajuz: إذا ما حلا في العين يا ليت ذا ليا

verses.sadr: حلفنا لهم والخيل تردي بنا معا

verses.ajuz: على رمة من ذي العظام تفاديا

verses.sadr: وقلت لمن قد أحضر الموت نفسه
verses.ajuz: ألا من لأمر حازم قد بدا ليا

verses.sadr: وقلت لهم ردوا المغيرة عن هوى

verses.ajuz: أرى الدهر لا ينجي من الموت ناجيا

translationEn: May God requite the ancient crumbling ruins — and may God requite your memory that recalls the bygone years. And your saying of what you cannot attain, when it looks sweet to the eye: 'Would that this were mine!' We defended our women at al-Furoq, sweeping away from them the blazing engulfing forces. We swore to them while our horses galloped together: we shall not leave them until they drop their spear-tips. High-raised blue-grey lances from Rudayna's spears — the growl of dogs warding off serpents. You scattered as old mangy she-camels scatter together at a carcass of old bones in avoidance. Did you not know that the spear-points protected our remnant — if only fate left anyone remaining? We guard the private quarters of our women and we are wary lest they encounter one day any disgrace. We have refused and refuse that your gums should clamp upon lips like soft-mouthed gazelles stooping to drink. I said to those who had brought their very selves to death: is there any man for a resolute matter that has appeared to me? I said to them: turn the raiders back from the ardor of their race-horses and seize their forelocks. We lead horses whose heads resemble the heads of women who find no one to protect them. They found us at al-Furoq to be no rabble, no scattered ones — nor were we called clients. Come to what you know — for I see that fate saves no one from death.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the ancient crumbling, dilapidated (ruins)
the bygone, the long-passed (years)
al-Furoq, a place name where the battle occurred
to sweep away, to remove from sight
the spear-tips, the upper ends of spears
a rabble, a mixed undisciplined mob
clients, freedmen (those without tribal standing)

context: The final poem of this batch opens with one of Arabic poetry's great existential complaints about the ruins and the futility of longing for what cannot be attained. The pivot to the battle of al-Furoq shows Banu Abs defending their women against raiders. The poem closes with a grim philosophical statement: fate saves no one from death — Antara's most sombre confession of mortality.
